title: Detect cycle in an undirected graph (DFS).
description: Detect cycle in an undirected graph (DFS).

examples.explanation: Step 1: Check vertex 0 (neighbors: 1,3). Check pair (1,3): Are 1 and 3 connected? DFS from 1 ignoring 0: 1→2→3. Yes connected. Cycle found. Return True.

Step 1: Check vertex 0 (neighbors: 1 only, no pairs). Step 2: Check vertex 1 (neighbors: 0,2). Check pair (0,2): DFS from 0 ignoring 1: 0 has no other neighbors. Not connected. Step 3: Check vertex 2 (neighbors: 1 only). No cycle found. Return False.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(V^2)
approaches.summary: For each vertex, check all pairs of its adjacent vertices to see if they are connected, indicating a cycle. This is inefficient and not practical for large graphs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Detect Cycle in Undirected Graph using Brute Force O(V²) Approach
ladders.desc: Complete implementation checking all vertex pairs for cycles in an undirected graph.
ladders.explanation: This brute force approach detects cycles in an undirected graph by examining every vertex and checking if any two of its adjacent vertices are connected to each other through another path, forming a triangle-like structure. The intuition is that in an undirected graph, if vertex A has neighbors B and C, and B and C are also connected (either directly or through other vertices), then A-B-C-A forms a cycle. We implement this by iterating through all vertices, and for each vertex, checking all pairs of its neighbors to see if they're connected via DFS/BFS. The algorithm works because any cycle of length ≥3 will have at least one vertex where two neighbors are connected. We maintain a visited array to avoid infinite loops during connectivity checks. Time complexity is O(V²) in worst case because for each vertex (V), we check all neighbor pairs (up to V² in dense graphs). Space complexity is O(V+E) for adjacency list and recursion stack. This connects to L1 by using the neighbor connectivity check as the core subroutine. Common mistakes include not handling self-loops properly, forgetting to mark visited nodes during connectivity checks, and incorrectly assuming direct edges between neighbors (must check connectivity through paths). Real code pattern: For each vertex u, get neighbors list. For each pair (v,w) in neighbors, check if v and w are connected via DFS/BFS ignoring u. If connected, cycle exists.

examples.trace: Step 1: Check vertex 0 (neighbors: 1,3). Check pair (1,3): Are 1 and 3 connected? DFS from 1 ignoring 0: 1→2→3. Yes connected. Cycle found. Return True.

Step 1: Check vertex 0 (neighbors: 1 only, no pairs). Step 2: Check vertex 1 (neighbors: 0,2). Check pair (0,2): DFS from 0 ignoring 1: 0 has no other neighbors. Not connected. Step 3: Check vertex 2 (neighbors: 1 only). No cycle found. Return False.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Check Connectivity Between Two Vertices Ignoring a Specific Vertex
ladders.desc: Determine if two vertices are connected in a graph when ignoring a particular vertex.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine checks whether two vertices v and w are connected in an undirected graph while temporarily ignoring a specific vertex u (typically the vertex whose neighbors we're checking). The intuition is that when checking for cycles involving vertex u, we want to see if u's neighbors v and w are connected through some path that doesn't include u itself, forming a cycle u-v-...-w-u. We use DFS or BFS starting from v, avoiding vertex u, and see if we can reach w. The algorithm marks u as visited initially to prevent traversal through it. Time complexity is O(V+E) for the DFS/BFS. Space complexity is O(V) for visited array and recursion stack. This connects to L0 as the core check performed for each vertex's neighbor pairs. Common mistakes include not properly avoiding vertex u (must mark it visited before DFS), not resetting visited array between checks, and not handling disconnected graphs. Real code pattern: function isConnected(v, w, u): visited[u]=True, perform DFS from v avoiding visited nodes, return True if w reached.

examples.input: Graph: 0-1-2-3, check if 1 and 3 connected ignoring vertex 2
examples.trace: Step 1: Mark vertex 2 as visited. Step 2: DFS from 1: neighbors [0,2]. 2 visited, go to 0. Step 3: From 0: neighbors [1]. All visited. DFS ends. 3 not reached. Return False.

examples.input: Graph: 0-1-2-3, check if 1 and 3 connected ignoring vertex 0
examples.trace: Step 1: Mark vertex 0 as visited. Step 2: DFS from 1: neighbors [0,2]. 0 visited, go to 2. Step 3: From 2: neighbors [1,3]. 1 visited, go to 3. Step 4: Reached 3. Return True.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Checking All Pairs of a Vertex's Neighbors for Connectivity
ladders.desc: For a given vertex, examine every pair of its neighbors to detect potential cycles.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves taking a vertex u, getting all its adjacent vertices (neighbors), and checking every possible pair (v,w) of these neighbors to see if they're connected (ignoring u). The intuition is that if any two neighbors of u are connected to each other through some path that doesn't include u, then u-v-...-w-u forms a cycle. We need to check all pairs because the cycle might involve any two neighbors. The algorithm generates all combinations of neighbors (n choose 2 pairs) and applies the connectivity check from L1. Time complexity for one vertex is O(d² * (V+E)) where d is degree, but worst case O(V³) when degree is V-1. Space complexity is O(V) for DFS. This connects to L1 by using the connectivity check repeatedly for each pair. Common mistakes include not checking all pairs (missing some combinations), not handling vertices with fewer than 2 neighbors (no pairs to check), and inefficient pair generation. Real code pattern: neighbors = adj[u]; for i in range(len(neighbors)): for j in range(i+1, len(neighbors)): if isConnected(neighbors[i], neighbors[j], u): return True.

examples.input: Vertex 0 with neighbors [1,2,3] in graph where 1-2 connected
examples.trace: Step 1: Get pairs: (1,2), (1,3), (2,3). Step 2: Check (1,2): isConnected(1,2,0) returns True (1-2 direct edge). Cycle found. Return True.

examples.input: Vertex 0 with neighbors [1,2] in graph where 1 and 2 not connected
examples.trace: Step 1: Get pairs: (1,2) only. Step 2: Check (1,2): isConnected(1,2,0) returns False. No cycle. Return False.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation and Neighbor Retrieval
ladders.desc: Represent an undirected graph and retrieve all neighbors of a vertex.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves creating a graph data structure (typically adjacency list) and implementing a function to get all vertices adjacent to a given vertex. The intuition is that we need efficient access to a vertex's neighbors to check pairs. An adjacency list stores for each vertex a list of directly connected vertices. For undirected graphs, each edge appears twice (u in v's list and v in u's list). The algorithm builds the list by iterating through edges and adding both directions. Time complexity to build is O(V+E), to get neighbors is O(1) average. Space complexity is O(V+E). This connects to L2 by providing the neighbor lists for pair checking. Common mistakes include not adding both directions for undirected edges, using adjacency matrix (wastes space), and not handling duplicate edges. Real code pattern: adj = [[] for _ in range(V)]; for u,v in edges: adj[u].append(v); adj[v].append(u). Get neighbors: return adj[u].

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize [[],[],[]]. Step 2: Add edge [0,1]: adj[0].append(1); adj[1].append(0). Step 3: Add edge [1,2]: adj[1].append(2); adj[2].append(1). Result: [[1],[0,2],[1]].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Depth-First Search (DFS) Traversal
ladders.desc: Traverse a graph using DFS to explore all connected vertices.
ladders.explanation: DFS is a fundamental graph traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. The intuition is to mimic exploring a maze by always taking the first available path until hitting a dead end, then backtracking. We use recursion or a stack to track vertices. The algorithm starts at a source vertex, marks it visited, then recursively visits all unvisited neighbors. Time complexity is O(V+E) as each vertex and edge processed once. Space complexity is O(V) for visited array and recursion stack. This connects to L3 as the traversal method used in connectivity checks. Common mistakes include not marking visited before recursion (causes infinite loops), not handling disconnected graphs, and stack overflow in deep recursion. Real code pattern: def dfs(v): visited[v]=True; for neighbor in adj[v]: if not visited[neighbor]: dfs(neighbor).

examples.trace: Step 1: Start at 0, mark visited. Step 2: Neighbors of 0: [1]. 1 not visited, go to 1. Step 3: At 1, mark visited. Neighbors: [0,2]. 0 visited, go to 2. Step 4: At 2, mark visited. Neighbors: [1]. 1 visited. Backtrack to 1, then 0. Done.

Step 1: Start at 0, mark visited. Step 2: Neighbors: [1,2]. Suppose go to 1 first. Step 3: At 1, mark visited. Neighbors: [0]. 0 visited. Backtrack to 0. Step 4: From 0, next neighbor 2. At 2, mark visited. Neighbors: [0]. 0 visited. Done.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Basics: Vertices, Edges, and Adjacency
ladders.desc: Understand fundamental graph concepts: vertices, edges, and adjacency relationships.
ladders.explanation: A graph is a collection of vertices (nodes) and edges (connections between vertices). In an undirected graph, edges have no direction—if vertex A is connected to vertex B, then B is also connected to A. Adjacency refers to the relationship between vertices that are directly connected by an edge. The intuition is modeling relationships like social networks (vertices as people, edges as friendships). Key concepts: vertex degree (number of edges incident to it), path (sequence of vertices where each adjacent pair is connected by an edge), and connectivity (whether a path exists between two vertices). This foundation connects to L4 as the basic structure DFS operates on. Common mistakes include confusing directed/undirected graphs, misunderstanding adjacency (must be direct connection), and not visualizing graphs properly. Real code pattern: Represent vertices as integers 0 to V-1; edges as pairs (u,v).

examples.output: Adjacency: 0 adjacent to 1; 1 adjacent to 0 and 2; 2 adjacent to 1
examples.trace: Step 1: List vertices: 0,1,2. Step 2: Edge (0,1): 0 and 1 are adjacent. Step 3: Edge (1,2): 1 and 2 are adjacent. Step 4: No edge between 0 and 2, so not adjacent.

examples.output: Adjacency: A adjacent to B and C; B adjacent to A; C adjacent to A
examples.trace: Step 1: Map to integers: A=0,B=1,C=2. Step 2: Edge (0,1): 0-1 adjacent. Step 3: Edge (0,2): 0-2 adjacent. Step 4: No edge 1-2, so not adjacent.

approaches.name: DFS with Parent Tracking O(V + E)
approaches.summary: Perform DFS traversal while keeping track of the parent node to avoid revisiting the immediate parent. If a visited node is encountered that is not the parent, a cycle is detected.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Detect Cycle in Undirected Graph using DFS with Parent Tracking
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to detect cycles in an undirected graph using DFS while tracking parent nodes.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires detecting cycles in an undirected graph using DFS with parent tracking. The intuition is that during DFS traversal, if we encounter a visited node that is not the immediate parent of the current node, we've found a back edge indicating a cycle. This works because in undirected graphs, edges are bidirectional, so we must avoid mistaking the edge back to the parent as a cycle. The algorithm performs DFS from each unvisited node, passing the parent information to avoid revisiting it. If any DFS call returns true (cycle detected), the overall function returns true. Time complexity is O(V + E) as we visit each vertex and edge once. Space complexity is O(V) for visited array and recursion stack. Connection to L1: This level integrates the DFS helper function with graph iteration. Common mistakes include not handling disconnected graphs, forgetting to pass parent parameter, or incorrectly checking for parent equality. Real code pattern: def hasCycle(graph): visited = [False]*V; for v in range(V): if not visited[v]: if dfs(v, -1): return True; return False; def dfs(node, parent): visited[node]=True; for neighbor in graph[node]: if not visited[neighbor]: if dfs(neighbor, node): return True; elif neighbor != parent: return True; return False.

examples.trace: Start at vertex 0 (unvisited). DFS(0, -1): Mark 0 visited. Neighbors: 1,3. Check neighbor 1: unvisited, call DFS(1,0). DFS(1,0): Mark 1 visited. Neighbors: 0,2. Check neighbor 0: visited but is parent (0), skip. Check neighbor 2: unvisited, call DFS(2,1). DFS(2,1): Mark 2 visited. Neighbors: 1,3. Check neighbor 1: visited but is parent (1), skip. Check neighbor 3: unvisited, call DFS(3,2). DFS(3,2): Mark 3 visited. Neighbors: 2,0. Check neighbor 2: visited but is parent (2), skip. Check neighbor 0: visited and not parent (2≠0), cycle detected, return True. Propagate True back through calls to top, overall returns True.

Start at vertex 0 (unvisited). DFS(0, -1): Mark 0 visited. Neighbors: 1. Check neighbor 1: unvisited, call DFS(1,0). DFS(1,0): Mark 1 visited. Neighbors: 0,2. Check neighbor 0: visited but is parent (0), skip. Check neighbor 2: unvisited, call DFS(2,1). DFS(2,1): Mark 2 visited. Neighbors: 1. Check neighbor 1: visited but is parent (1), skip. Return False. Back to DFS(1,0): neighbor 2 returned False, return False. Back to DFS(0,-1): neighbor 1 returned False, return False. All vertices visited, overall returns False.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Helper Function with Parent Parameter
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive DFS function that detects cycles by checking visited neighbors against parent.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core DFS function that traverses the graph recursively while tracking the parent node. The intuition is that during DFS, we need to distinguish between revisiting the parent (which is allowed in undirected graphs) and revisiting another visited node (which indicates a cycle). The function marks the current node as visited, then iterates through its neighbors. For each neighbor: if unvisited, recursively call DFS with current node as parent; if visited and not equal to parent, return true (cycle detected). The parent parameter prevents false cycle detection from the edge back to where we came from. Time complexity for this function on its subgraph is O(V' + E') where V' and E' are vertices and edges in the connected component. Space complexity is O(V') for recursion depth. Connection to L0: This function is called from the main loop for each unvisited vertex. Connection to L2: This implements the cycle detection logic. Common mistakes: forgetting to return the recursive call result, or incorrect parent comparison. Real code pattern: def dfs(node, parent): visited[node]=True; for neighbor in graph[node]: if not visited[neighbor]: if dfs(neighbor, node): return True; elif neighbor != parent: return True; return False.

examples.input: Start DFS at node 0 with parent -1, graph: 0-1, 1-2, 2-0
examples.trace: DFS(0,-1): Mark 0 visited. Neighbors: 1. Check neighbor 1: unvisited, call DFS(1,0). DFS(1,0): Mark 1 visited. Neighbors: 0,2. Check neighbor 0: visited and is parent (0), skip. Check neighbor 2: unvisited, call DFS(2,1). DFS(2,1): Mark 2 visited. Neighbors: 1,0. Check neighbor 1: visited and is parent (1), skip. Check neighbor 0: visited and not parent (1≠0), return True. Propagate True back to DFS(1,0) and DFS(0,-1), overall True.

examples.input: Start DFS at node 0 with parent -1, graph: 0-1, 1-2
examples.trace: DFS(0,-1): Mark 0 visited. Neighbors: 1. Check neighbor 1: unvisited, call DFS(1,0). DFS(1,0): Mark 1 visited. Neighbors: 0,2. Check neighbor 0: visited and is parent (0), skip. Check neighbor 2: unvisited, call DFS(2,1). DFS(2,1): Mark 2 visited. Neighbors: 1. Check neighbor 1: visited and is parent (1), skip. Return False. Back to DFS(1,0): neighbor 2 returned False, return False. Back to DFS(0,-1): neighbor 1 returned False, return False.

testCases.input: Start at 0, parent -1, graph: 0-1, 1-0 (self-loop via two edges)
Start at 0, parent -1, graph: single node 0

ladders.title: Core Logic — Cycle Detection via Back Edge Check
ladders.desc: Identify when a visited neighbor that is not the parent indicates a cycle.
ladders.explanation: This core logic focuses on the key condition that detects cycles: during DFS traversal, if we encounter a neighbor that has already been visited AND that neighbor is not the immediate parent of the current node, then we have found a back edge, indicating a cycle. The intuition is that in an undirected graph without cycles (a tree), the only visited neighbor we should encounter is the parent. Any other visited neighbor means there exists an alternative path between nodes, forming a cycle. This check must be performed for each neighbor during DFS. The parent tracking is essential because the edge to the parent is not a back edge—it's the edge we just traversed. Time complexity for this check is O(1) per edge. Space complexity is O(1) additional. Connection to L1: This is the conditional check inside the DFS loop. Connection to L3: This relies on visited tracking. Common mistakes: checking neighbor == parent instead of neighbor != parent, or missing the check entirely. Real code pattern: for neighbor in graph[node]: if not visited[neighbor]: # recursive call; elif neighbor != parent: return True.

examples.input: At node 2 with parent 1, neighbors: [1,3,0], visited: {0,1,2}, check neighbor 0
examples.trace: Current node: 2, parent: 1. Neighbors: 1,3,0. Check neighbor 1: visited and equals parent (1=1), skip. Check neighbor 3: unvisited, handle recursively. Check neighbor 0: visited and not parent (0≠1), condition neighbor != parent is true, so return True immediately.

examples.input: At node 1 with parent 0, neighbors: [0,2], visited: {0,1}, check neighbor 0
examples.trace: Current node: 1, parent: 0. Neighbors: 0,2. Check neighbor 0: visited and equals parent (0=0), skip. Check neighbor 2: unvisited, handle recursively. No cycle detected from this check.

testCases.input: Node: 0, parent: -1, neighbors: [1,2], visited: {0}, check neighbor 1
Node: 1, parent: 0, neighbors: [0,2], visited: {0,1}, check neighbor 0
Node: 2, parent: 1, neighbors: [1,0], visited: {0,1,2}, check neighbor 0
Node: 3, parent: 2, neighbors: [2,4], visited: {2,3}, check neighbor 2
Node: 4, parent: 3, neighbors: [3,5,0], visited: {0,3,4}, check neighbor 0
Node: 0, parent: -1, neighbors: [1], visited: {0}, check neighbor 1
Node: 5, parent: 4, neighbors: [4,6], visited: {4,5}, check neighbor 4
Node: 6, parent: 5, neighbors: [5,7,2], visited: {2,5,6}, check neighbor 2
Node: 7, parent: 6, neighbors: [6,8], visited: {6,7}, check neighbor 6
Node: 8, parent: 7, neighbors: [7,9,1], visited: {1,7,8}, check neighbor 1

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Traversal with Visited Tracking
ladders.desc: Perform DFS traversal on a graph while marking nodes as visited to avoid reprocessing.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves traversing a graph using Depth-First Search (DFS) while maintaining a visited array to track which nodes have been processed. The intuition is that in graph traversal, we must avoid revisiting nodes to prevent infinite loops and ensure each node is processed once. DFS explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. The visited array (typically boolean) marks nodes when first encountered. For each node, we recursively visit all unvisited neighbors. This is fundamental to many graph algorithms. Time complexity is O(V + E) as each node and edge is processed once. Space complexity is O(V) for visited array and recursion stack. Connection to L2: This traversal provides the context where cycle detection occurs. Connection to L4: This uses iteration over neighbors. Common mistakes: not marking node as visited before recursing (causing infinite recursion), or using visited incorrectly across disconnected components. Real code pattern: visited = [False]*V; def dfs(node): visited[node]=True; for neighbor in graph[node]: if not visited[neighbor]: dfs(neighbor).

examples.trace: Initialize visited = [F,F,F]. Start dfs(0): mark visited[0]=True. Neighbors of 0: [1]. Check neighbor 1: not visited, call dfs(1). dfs(1): mark visited[1]=True. Neighbors of 1: [0,2]. Check neighbor 0: visited, skip. Check neighbor 2: not visited, call dfs(2). dfs(2): mark visited[2]=True. Neighbors of 2: [1]. Check neighbor 1: visited, skip. Return. Back to dfs(1): done. Back to dfs(0): done. Final visited: [T,T,T].

Initialize visited = [F,F,F]. Start dfs(0): mark visited[0]=True. Neighbors of 0: [1,2]. Suppose order: first neighbor 1. Check neighbor 1: not visited, call dfs(1). dfs(1): mark visited[1]=True. Neighbors of 1: [0]. Check neighbor 0: visited, skip. Return. Back to dfs(0): next neighbor 2. Check neighbor 2: not visited, call dfs(2). dfs(2): mark visited[2]=True. Neighbors of 2: [0]. Check neighbor 0: visited, skip. Return. Back to dfs(0): done. Final visited: [T,T,T].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterate Over Graph Neighbors
ladders.desc: Access and process all neighbors of a node in a graph representation.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves iterating through all neighbors of a given node in a graph. Graphs are typically represented using adjacency lists (list of lists) or adjacency matrices. For adjacency list, each node has a list of its adjacent nodes. Iteration involves looping through this list to access each neighbor. This is fundamental because graph algorithms often require processing all edges incident to a node. The intuition is that to explore connections, we need to examine each neighbor. Time complexity is O(degree(node)) per iteration. Space complexity is O(1) additional. Connection to L3: This iteration is used in DFS traversal to visit neighbors. Connection to L5: This relies on understanding graph representations. Common mistakes: iterating beyond list bounds, or misunderstanding directed vs undirected neighbors. Real code pattern: for neighbor in graph[node]: # process neighbor.

examples.trace: Graph representation: graph = [[1,2,3], ...]. For node 0, graph[0] = [1,2,3]. Iteration: first neighbor = 1, process it; second neighbor = 2, process it; third neighbor = 3, process it; end of list.
Graph representation: graph = [..., [0,2], ...]. For node 1, graph[1] = [0,2]. Iteration: first neighbor = 0, process it; second neighbor = 2, process it; end of list.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Representation as Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Understand how an undirected graph is represented using adjacency lists.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept covers representing an undirected graph using an adjacency list. An undirected graph consists of vertices (nodes) and edges connecting pairs of vertices. In adjacency list representation, we maintain a list for each vertex containing all vertices adjacent to it. For example, if there's an edge between vertex 0 and vertex 1, then 1 appears in the list for 0, and 0 appears in the list for 1. This representation is space-efficient for sparse graphs (O(V + E)) and allows quick access to neighbors. The intuition is that it captures connectivity directly. Time to access neighbors is proportional to the degree of the node. Space is O(V + E). Connection to L4: This representation enables neighbor iteration. Common mistakes: forgetting to add edges in both directions for undirected graphs, or using incorrect indices. Real code pattern: graph = [[] for _ in range(V)]; for edge in edges: u,v = edge; graph[u].append(v); graph[v].append(u).

examples.trace: Initialize graph with 3 empty lists: [[], [], []]. Add edge 0-1: append 1 to graph[0] → [[1], [], []]; append 0 to graph[1] → [[1], [0], []]. Add edge 1-2: append 2 to graph[1] → [[1], [0,2], []]; append 1 to graph[2] → [[1], [0,2], [1]]. Final: [[1], [0,2], [1]].

Initialize graph with 4 empty lists: [[], [], [], []]. Add edge 0-1: append 1 to graph[0] → [[1], [], [], []]; append 0 to graph[1] → [[1], [0], [], []]. Add edge 0-2: append 2 to graph[0] → [[1,2], [0], [], []]; append 0 to graph[2] → [[1,2], [0], [0], []]. Add edge 1-3: append 3 to graph[1] → [[1,2], [0,3], [0], []]; append 1 to graph[3] → [[1,2], [0,3], [0], [1]]. Final: [[1,2], [0,3], [0], [1]].

approaches.name: BFS with Parent Tracking O(V + E)
approaches.summary: Use BFS traversal, tracking the parent of each node to prevent back edges. If a visited node is found that is not the parent, it indicates a cycle.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Detect Cycle in Undirected Graph using BFS with Parent Tracking
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of cycle detection in an undirected graph using BFS traversal with parent node tracking.
ladders.explanation: This solution detects cycles in undirected graphs by performing BFS traversal while tracking each node's parent. The intuition is that in BFS, when we encounter a visited node, we must check if it's the parent of the current node. In undirected graphs, each edge creates a bidirectional connection, so when traversing from node A to node B, B's parent is A. If we later encounter B from another node C (where C is not A), and B is already visited, this indicates a cycle because there are two different paths to reach B. The algorithm works by: 1) Initializing visited array and parent tracking, 2) Performing BFS from each unvisited node (to handle disconnected graphs), 3) During BFS, for each neighbor, if not visited, mark visited and set parent, then enqueue; if visited and not the parent, cycle detected. Time complexity is O(V + E) as we visit each vertex and edge once. Space complexity is O(V) for queue, visited array, and parent tracking. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete BFS traversal with cycle detection logic. Common mistakes include: forgetting to handle disconnected graphs, incorrectly checking parent relationship (must check neighbor != parent[current]), and not marking nodes as visited when enqueuing. Real code pattern: def hasCycle(graph): visited = [False]*V; for v in range(V): if not visited[v]: if bfsCycle(v): return True; return False.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start BFS from vertex 0. visited[0]=True, parent[0]=-1, queue=[0]. Step 2: Process 0, neighbors: 1,3. neighbor 1 not visited: visited[1]=True, parent[1]=0, queue=[1,3]. neighbor 3 not visited: visited[3]=True, parent[3]=0, queue=[1,3]. Step 3: Process 1, neighbors: 0,2. neighbor 0 visited and parent[1]=0 (equal), skip. neighbor 2 not visited: visited[2]=True, parent[2]=1, queue=[3,2]. Step 4: Process 3, neighbors: 2,0. neighbor 2 visited and parent[3]=0 (2 != 0), CYCLE DETECTED between 3-2-1-0-3. Return True.

Step 1: Start BFS from vertex 0. visited[0]=True, parent[0]=-1, queue=[0]. Step 2: Process 0, neighbor 1 not visited: visited[1]=True, parent[1]=0, queue=[1]. Step 3: Process 1, neighbors: 0,2. neighbor 0 visited and parent[1]=0 (equal), skip. neighbor 2 not visited: visited[2]=True, parent[2]=1, queue=[2]. Step 4: Process 2, neighbor 1 visited and parent[2]=1 (equal), skip. Queue empty, no cycle detected. Return False.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — BFS Traversal with Parent Tracking for a Connected Component
ladders.desc: Implement BFS traversal that tracks parent nodes and detects cycles within a connected component.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine performs BFS starting from a given source node, tracking each node's parent to detect cycles within that connected component. The intuition is that BFS explores nodes level by level, and by remembering the parent (the node we came from), we can distinguish between the natural back edge to parent (which is not a cycle in undirected graphs) and a cycle-forming edge to an already visited non-parent node. The algorithm: 1) Initialize queue with source, mark visited, set parent to -1 for source. 2) While queue not empty: dequeue current node. 3) For each neighbor: if not visited, mark visited, set parent[neighbor]=current, enqueue neighbor; if visited and neighbor != parent[current], return True (cycle detected). 4) If queue empties without cycle, return False. Time complexity is O(V + E) for the component. Space complexity is O(V) for queue and parent array. This feeds into L0 by being the core function called for each connected component. Common mistakes: not setting parent correctly when visiting new nodes, forgetting to check neighbor != parent[current] before declaring cycle, and not marking nodes as visited when enqueuing (causing duplicates). Real code pattern: def bfsCycle(source): queue=[source]; visited[source]=True; parent[source]=-1; while queue: current=queue.pop(0); for neighbor in graph[current]: if not visited[neighbor]: visited[neighbor]=True; parent[neighbor]=current; queue.append(neighbor); elif neighbor != parent[current]: return True; return False.

examples.input: Start from vertex 0 in graph: edges from 0: [1,3]; from 1: [0,2]; from 2: [1,3]; from 3: [2,0]
examples.trace: Step 1: queue=[0], visited[0]=True, parent[0]=-1. Step 2: current=0, neighbors 1,3. neighbor 1 not visited: visited[1]=True, parent[1]=0, queue=[1,3]. neighbor 3 not visited: visited[3]=True, parent[3]=0, queue=[1,3]. Step 3: current=1, neighbors 0,2. neighbor 0 visited and parent[1]=0 (equal), skip. neighbor 2 not visited: visited[2]=True, parent[2]=1, queue=[3,2]. Step 4: current=3, neighbors 2,0. neighbor 2 visited and parent[3]=0 (2 != 0), CYCLE DETECTED. Return True.

examples.input: Start from vertex 0 in graph: edges from 0: [1]; from 1: [0,2]; from 2: [1]
examples.trace: Step 1: queue=[0], visited[0]=True, parent[0]=-1. Step 2: current=0, neighbor 1 not visited: visited[1]=True, parent[1]=0, queue=[1]. Step 3: current=1, neighbors 0,2. neighbor 0 visited and parent[1]=0 (equal), skip. neighbor 2 not visited: visited[2]=True, parent[2]=1, queue=[2]. Step 4: current=2, neighbor 1 visited and parent[2]=1 (equal), skip. Queue empty, no cycle. Return False.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Detecting Cycle by Checking Visited Non-Parent Nodes in BFS
ladders.desc: Identify the key condition that indicates a cycle during BFS traversal in undirected graphs.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that during BFS traversal of an undirected graph, a cycle exists if and only if we encounter a visited node that is not the parent of the current node. In undirected graphs, edges are bidirectional, so when we traverse from node A to node B, B is marked as visited and A is recorded as B's parent. If later, from another node C, we encounter B again (and B is already visited), and C is not B's parent, this means there are two distinct paths from the starting point to B: one through A and another through C, forming a cycle. The parent check is crucial because the immediate back edge to the parent (A→B then B→A) does not indicate a cycle—it's just the bidirectional nature of the edge. The logic: during BFS, for each neighbor of current node: if neighbor is visited AND neighbor ≠ parent[current], return True (cycle detected). This works because BFS explores level by level, ensuring we detect cycles regardless of their size. Time complexity for this check is O(1) per edge. Space complexity is O(1) additional for the comparison. This feeds into L1 as the critical condition within the BFS loop. Common mistakes: forgetting to check neighbor != parent[current], incorrectly implementing parent tracking, or misunderstanding that in undirected graphs, all edges are effectively two-directional. Real code pattern: if visited[neighbor] and neighbor != parent[current]: return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: Check condition: visited[2] is True. Step 2: Check if neighbor != parent[current]: 2 != parent[3] (which is 0). Step 3: Since both conditions true, this indicates a cycle. Explanation: Node 2 was visited earlier from a different path (not from node 3), showing two paths to 2.

Step 1: Check condition: visited[0] is True. Step 2: Check if neighbor != parent[current]: 0 != parent[1] (which is 0) is False. Step 3: Since neighbor equals parent, this is just the back edge to parent, not a cycle. Skip.

ladders.title: Building Block — BFS Traversal with Visited Tracking in Graphs
ladders.desc: Implement standard BFS traversal for graphs while maintaining a visited array to avoid reprocessing nodes.
ladders.explanation: This building block implements Breadth-First Search (BFS) traversal for graph structures, which is fundamental for the cycle detection approach. BFS explores nodes level by level, starting from a source node, using a queue data structure. The intuition is to visit all nodes reachable from the source in order of their distance, ensuring we don't revisit nodes by tracking visited status. The algorithm: 1) Initialize queue with source node. 2) Mark source as visited. 3) While queue not empty: dequeue current node, process it (e.g., print or check), for each unvisited neighbor: mark visited, enqueue neighbor. This ensures each node is processed exactly once. Time complexity is O(V + E) as each vertex and edge is processed once. Space complexity is O(V) for queue and visited array. This feeds into L2 by providing the traversal framework where parent tracking and cycle checks can be added. Common mistakes: not marking nodes as visited when enqueuing (leading to duplicates), using stack instead of queue (making it DFS), forgetting to handle disconnected graphs in full implementation, and not initializing visited array properly. Real code pattern: def bfs(source): queue=[source]; visited[source]=True; while queue: current=queue.pop(0); for neighbor in graph[current]: if not visited[neighbor]: visited[neighbor]=True; queue.append(neighbor).

examples.input: Start from vertex 0 in graph: edges from 0: [1,2]; from 1: [0,3]; from 2: [0,3]; from 3: [1,2]
examples.trace: Step 1: queue=[0], visited[0]=True. Step 2: current=0, neighbors 1,2. neighbor 1 not visited: visited[1]=True, queue=[1,2]. neighbor 2 not visited: visited[2]=True, queue=[1,2]. Step 3: current=1, neighbors 0,3. neighbor 0 visited, skip. neighbor 3 not visited: visited[3]=True, queue=[2,3]. Step 4: current=2, neighbors 0,3. neighbor 0 visited, skip. neighbor 3 visited, skip. Step 5: current=3, neighbors 1,2, both visited, skip. Queue empty. BFS order is dequeue order: 0,1,2,3.

examples.input: Start from vertex 0 in graph: edges from 0: [1]; from 1: [0,2]; from 2: [1]
examples.trace: Step 1: queue=[0], visited[0]=True. Step 2: current=0, neighbor 1 not visited: visited[1]=True, queue=[1]. Step 3: current=1, neighbors 0,2. neighbor 0 visited, skip. neighbor 2 not visited: visited[2]=True, queue=[2]. Step 4: current=2, neighbor 1 visited, skip. Queue empty. BFS order: 0,1,2.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Queue Operations for BFS Traversal
ladders.desc: Implement and use queue operations (enqueue, dequeue) to manage nodes during BFS traversal.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation focuses on using a queue data structure to manage the order of node processing in BFS. A queue follows First-In-First-Out (FIFO) order, which is essential for BFS to explore nodes level by level. The intuition is that by adding newly discovered nodes to the back of the queue and processing nodes from the front, we ensure that all nodes at distance d are processed before nodes at distance d+1. Key operations: enqueue (add to back) for newly discovered nodes, dequeue (remove from front) to get the next node to process, and check if empty. In Python, list can be used with append() for enqueue and pop(0) for dequeue, or collections.deque for efficiency. Time complexity for enqueue/dequeue is O(1) with proper implementation. Space complexity is O(V) for storing nodes in queue. This feeds into L3 as the mechanism that drives the BFS traversal order. Common mistakes: using stack operations (push/pop) which makes it DFS, not checking queue emptiness leading to infinite loops, inefficient dequeue from list front (O(n) if using pop(0) on large lists), and not initializing queue properly. Real code pattern: queue = []; queue.append(item) # enqueue; item = queue.pop(0) # dequeue; while queue: # process.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize empty queue []. Step 2: Enqueue 0: queue=[0]. Step 3: Enqueue 1: queue=[0,1]. Step 4: Enqueue 2: queue=[0,1,2]. Step 5: Enqueue 3: queue=[0,1,2,3]. Step 6: Dequeue: remove front 0, queue=[1,2,3]. Step 7: Dequeue: remove front 1, queue=[2,3]. Step 8: Dequeue: remove front 2, queue=[3]. Step 9: Dequeue: remove front 3, queue=[]. Dequeue order is 0,1,2,3.

examples.input: Enqueue nodes: 5,3,7; then dequeue twice; then enqueue 2
examples.trace: Step 1: Start queue=[]. Step 2: Enqueue 5: queue=[5]. Step 3: Enqueue 3: queue=[5,3]. Step 4: Enqueue 7: queue=[5,3,7]. Step 5: Dequeue: remove 5, queue=[3,7]. Step 6: Dequeue: remove 3, queue=[7]. Step 7: Enqueue 2: queue=[7,2]. Final queue: [7,2].

testCases.input: Enqueue: 7,8,9; Dequeue twice; Enqueue 10; Dequeue all
Queue starts with [2,4,6]; Dequeue; Enqueue 8; Dequeue all
Queue starts with [9]; Enqueue 11; Dequeue; Enqueue 13

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Representation as Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Understand and create adjacency list representation for undirected graphs.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept covers representing an undirected graph using an adjacency list, which is essential for the BFS approach. An undirected graph consists of vertices (nodes) and edges connecting pairs of vertices, with no direction (edges are bidirectional). Adjacency list representation stores, for each vertex, a list of its neighboring vertices. The intuition is that this provides efficient access to all edges incident to a vertex, which is crucial for graph traversal algorithms like BFS. To build it: 1) Initialize an empty list of size V (number of vertices). 2) For each edge (u,v), add v to the list of u and add u to the list of v (since undirected). This ensures that from any vertex, we can quickly find all connected vertices. Time complexity to build is O(E) for adding edges. Space complexity is O(V + E) to store lists. This feeds into L4 as the data structure that BFS operates on. Common mistakes: forgetting to add both directions for undirected graphs, using adjacency matrix for sparse graphs (inefficient), not initializing lists properly, and confusing with directed graphs (where edges are one-way). Real code pattern: V=5; graph=[[] for _ in range(V)]; for u,v in edges: graph[u].append(v); graph[v].append(u).

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize graph = [[],[],[],[]]. Step 2: Add edge (0,1): graph[0].append(1) → graph[0]=[1]; graph[1].append(0) → graph[1]=[0]. Step 3: Add edge (1,2): graph[1].append(2) → graph[1]=[0,2]; graph[2].append(1) → graph[2]=[1]. Step 4: Add edge (2,3): graph[2].append(3) → graph[2]=[1,3]; graph[3].append(2) → graph[3]=[2]. Final: 0:[1], 1:[0,2], 2:[1,3], 3:[2].

Step 1: Initialize graph = [[],[],[]]. Step 2: Add edge (0,1): graph[0].append(1) → [1]; graph[1].append(0) → [0]. Step 3: Add edge (1,2): graph[1].append(2) → [0,2]; graph[2].append(1) → [1]. Step 4: Add edge (2,0): graph[2].append(0) → [1,0]; graph[0].append(2) → [1,2]. Final: 0:[1,2], 1:[0,2], 2:[1,0].

approaches.name: Union-Find (Disjoint Set) O(E * α(V))
approaches.summary: Iterate through all edges and use union-find to merge sets of vertices. If two vertices of an edge are already in the same set, a cycle is detected.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Detect Cycle in Undirected Graph Using Union-Find
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of cycle detection in an undirected graph using the Union-Find data structure.
ladders.explanation: The Union-Find approach detects cycles in undirected graphs by treating each vertex as a separate set and merging sets as we process edges. The intuition is that in an undirected graph, a cycle exists if we encounter an edge connecting two vertices that are already in the same connected component. We initialize each vertex as its own parent (representative of its set). For each edge (u, v), we find the root parents of both vertices. If they have the same root, adding this edge would create a cycle since both vertices are already connected through some path. If they have different roots, we union them by making one root point to the other, effectively merging the two sets. The time complexity is O(E * α(V)), where α is the inverse Ackermann function (effectively constant), and space is O(V) for parent and rank arrays. This approach is efficient because it processes edges in any order and avoids the overhead of DFS/BFS traversal. Common mistakes include not using path compression or union by rank, which leads to worse time complexity, or incorrectly handling self-loops (which always indicate cycles). The connection to L1 is that this full solution relies on the find and union operations implemented in the helper functions.

examples.trace: Initialize parent = [0,1,2,3], rank = [0,0,0,0]. Edge [0,1]: find(0)=0, find(1)=1, different roots → union(0,1): parent[1]=0, rank[0]=1. Edge [1,2]: find(1)=0 (since parent[1]=0, find(0)=0), find(2)=2, different → union(0,2): parent[2]=0, rank[0]=1 (unchanged as ranks equal). Edge [2,3]: find(2)=0, find(3)=3, different → union(0,3): parent[3]=0, rank[0]=1. Edge [3,0]: find(3)=0, find(0)=0, same root → cycle detected, return True.

Initialize parent = [0,1,2], rank = [0,0,0]. Edge [0,1]: find(0)=0, find(1)=1, different → union(0,1): parent[1]=0, rank[0]=1. Edge [1,2]: find(1)=0, find(2)=2, different → union(0,2): parent[2]=0, rank[0]=1. All edges processed, no cycle, return False.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Find and Union Operations with Path Compression and Union by Rank
ladders.desc: Implement the find (with path compression) and union (by rank) operations for the Union-Find data structure.
ladders.explanation: The find operation locates the root parent of a vertex, using recursion and path compression to flatten the tree for future efficiency. Path compression works by updating each visited node's parent to point directly to the root during the find call, reducing the tree height. The union operation merges two sets by attaching the root of the smaller rank (tree depth) under the root of the larger rank, keeping the tree balanced to optimize future finds. The intuition is that these optimizations make the operations nearly constant time per call, crucial for the overall O(E * α(V)) complexity. Step-by-step: find(x): if parent[x] != x, set parent[x] = find(parent[x]) recursively, then return parent[x]. union(x, y): find roots rootX and rootY; if same, do nothing; else, compare ranks: if rank[rootX] < rank[rootY], set parent[rootX] = rootY; if greater, set parent[rootY] = rootX; if equal, choose one as parent and increment its rank. Time per operation is O(α(V)), space O(V). This feeds into L0 by providing the core functions to process edges. Common mistakes include forgetting to update ranks correctly or not implementing path compression properly, leading to slower performance. Real code pattern: parent = list(range(n)), rank = [0]*n; def find(x): if parent[x] != x: parent[x] = find(parent[x]); return parent[x]; def union(x, y): rootX, rootY = find(x), find(y); if rootX == rootY: return; if rank[rootX] < rank[rootY]: parent[rootX] = rootY; elif rank[rootX] > rank[rootY]: parent[rootY] = rootX; else: parent[rootY] = rootX; rank[rootX] += 1.

examples.input: Initialize with 4 vertices, perform union(0,1), union(1,2), find(2)
examples.trace: Start: parent=[0,1,2,3], rank=[0,0,0,0]. union(0,1): find(0)=0, find(1)=1, ranks equal → parent[1]=0, rank[0]=1. union(1,2): find(1)=0 (path: parent[1]=0, find(0)=0), find(2)=2, rank[0]=1 > rank[2]=0 → parent[2]=0, rank unchanged. find(2): parent[2]=0, parent[0]=0 → return 0.

examples.input: Initialize with 3 vertices, perform union(0,1), union(0,2), find(1)
examples.trace: Start: parent=[0,1,2], rank=[0,0,0]. union(0,1): find(0)=0, find(1)=1, ranks equal → parent[1]=0, rank[0]=1. union(0,2): find(0)=0, find(2)=2, rank[0]=1 > rank[2]=0 → parent[2]=0. find(1): parent[1]=0, parent[0]=0 → return 0.

testCases.input: n=4, operations: union(0,1), union(1,2), union(2,3), find(3)
n=5, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(0,2), find(4)
n=5, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(0,2), find(3)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Detecting Cycle by Checking Edge Connectivity in Union-Find
ladders.desc: The central technique of iterating through edges and using union-find to detect if two vertices are already connected.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that in an undirected graph, a cycle forms when we add an edge between two vertices that are already in the same connected component. Using Union-Find, we maintain disjoint sets representing these components. As we process each edge (u, v), we check if find(u) == find(v). If true, the vertices are already connected via some path, so adding this edge creates a cycle. If false, we union(u, v) to merge their sets, indicating they are now connected. The intuition relies on the property that Union-Find efficiently tracks connectivity without explicitly storing paths. Step-by-step: initialize Union-Find for all vertices; for each edge (u, v): if find(u) == find(v), return True (cycle detected); else, union(u, v); after processing all edges, return False. Time is O(E * α(V)) for E edges, space O(V). This feeds into L1 by using the find and union operations. Common mistakes include processing edges in a way that misses cycles (e.g., not checking all edges) or misinterpreting the graph as directed. Real code pattern: for u, v in edges: if find(u) == find(v): return True; union(u, v); return False.

examples.trace: Initialize Union-Find. Edge [0,1]: find(0)=0, find(1)=1, different → union(0,1). Edge [1,2]: find(1)=0, find(2)=2, different → union(0,2). Edge [2,0]: find(2)=0, find(0)=0, same root → cycle detected.

Initialize Union-Find. Edge [0,1]: find(0)=0, find(1)=1, different → union(0,1). Edge [1,2]: find(1)=0, find(2)=2, different → union(0,2). Edge [2,3]: find(2)=0, find(3)=3, different → union(0,3). All edges processed, no same-root checks, so no cycle.

ladders.title: Building Block — Representing Graph as Edges and Initializing Union-Find
ladders.desc: Setting up the graph representation and initializing the Union-Find data structure for cycle detection.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves two parts: representing the undirected graph as a list of edges and initializing the Union-Find arrays. The graph is typically given as a number of vertices V and a list of edges, where each edge is a pair (u, v). Since the graph is undirected, edge (u, v) is the same as (v, u), and we assume the input handles this. For Union-Find, we initialize two arrays: parent and rank. The parent array starts with each vertex as its own parent (parent[i] = i), indicating each vertex is in its own set. The rank array starts with all zeros, representing the initial depth of each tree. The intuition is that this setup allows us to track connectivity dynamically as we process edges. Step-by-step: given V, create parent = list(range(V)) and rank = [0] * V. This feeds into L2 by providing the data structures needed for the cycle detection logic. Time for initialization is O(V), space O(V). Common mistakes include incorrect array sizes (e.g., not matching V) or misunderstanding that edges may be duplicated (which should be handled in the cycle check). Real code pattern: V = number of vertices; edges = list of pairs; parent = [i for i in range(V)]; rank = [0] * V.

examples.trace: Given V=4, create parent array: indices 0 to 3, set each to its index → [0,1,2,3]. Create rank array of length 4, all zeros → [0,0,0,0]. Graph edges are provided as list.
Given V=3, parent = [0,1,2], rank = [0,0,0]. Edges list includes a potential cycle.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Finding the Root Parent in a Tree Structure
ladders.desc: Implement a function to find the root parent of a node in a tree, without optimizations.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves traversing up a tree to find the root parent of a given node. In the context of Union-Find, each node has a parent pointer, and the root is the node that is its own parent (i.e., parent[root] = root). The intuition is that this root represents the set or connected component that the node belongs to. Without optimizations like path compression, we simply follow parent pointers recursively until we reach the root. Step-by-step: define find(x): if parent[x] == x, return x; else, return find(parent[x]). This is a recursive function that climbs the tree. Time complexity in the worst case is O(h), where h is the height of the tree, which can be O(V) without optimizations. Space is O(h) for recursion stack. This feeds into L3 as a simpler version of the optimized find used in Union-Find initialization. Common mistakes include infinite recursion if parent pointers are incorrect (e.g., cycles in parent links) or not handling base case properly. Real code pattern: def find(x): if parent[x] == x: return x; return find(parent[x]).

examples.trace: Start at node 3: parent[3]=2, so call find(2). At node 2: parent[2]=1, call find(1). At node 1: parent[1]=0, call find(0). At node 0: parent[0]=0, return 0. Unwind: find(1) returns 0, find(2) returns 0, find(3) returns 0.
Start at node 0: parent[0]=0, so return 0 immediately.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Parent-Child Relationships in Trees
ladders.desc: The simplest prerequisite: representing hierarchical connections using parent pointers.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept involves understanding how parent-child relationships work in tree structures. In a tree, each node (except the root) has exactly one parent, and the root has no parent (or is its own parent). We can represent this using an array where the index is the node and the value is its parent. For example, if parent[1] = 0, then node 1's parent is node 0. The intuition is that this allows us to model connections or groupings, such as in family trees or organizational charts. Step-by-step: given n nodes, create an array parent of size n. Initialize it so that each node is its own parent (parent[i] = i), meaning each node starts as a separate tree with itself as the root. This is the starting point for Union-Find. Time to initialize is O(n), space O(n). This feeds into L4 by providing the basic data structure for finding roots. Common mistakes include confusing parent with child relationships or not initializing correctly. Real code pattern: n = number of nodes; parent = [i for i in range(n)].

examples.trace: We have 3 nodes: 0, 1, 2. Set parent[0] = 0 (node 0 is its own parent), parent[1] = 1, parent[2] = 2. So array is [0,1,2].

examples.input: 5 nodes, set parent so node 1's parent is 0, node 2's parent is 1, others are self-parent
examples.trace: Start with self-parents: [0,1,2,3,4]. Update parent[1] = 0 → [0,0,2,3,4]. Update parent[2] = 1 → [0,0,1,3,4]. Nodes 3 and 4 remain self-parents.
